title: Andreas Wagner awarded ERC Advanced Grant
gist: SFI External Professor Andreas Wagner won a EUR 2.5 million, five-year ERC Advanced Grant for the SEASCAPES project, which will use genome editing, E. coli growth-rate experiments, and AI to map up to 100 trillion genotypes and resolve why Darwinian evolution reaches high-fitness peaks despite rugged, valley-separated fitness landscapes.

insights.claim: Andreas Wagner, an SFI External Professor at the University of Zurich, was awarded a European Research Council Advanced Grant of EUR 2.5 million over five years to fund the SEASCAPES project studying fitness landscapes in evolutionary biology.
insights.speaker: Santa Fe Institute
insights.so_what: Marks a large, competitively-won grant validating fitness-landscape research as a funding priority in evolutionary biology.
insights.locator: SFI External Professor Andreas Wagner (University of Zurich) was recently awarded

insights.claim: ERC Advanced Grants have become increasingly competitive in recent years as overall funding opportunities for researchers shrink.
insights.speaker: Santa Fe Institute
insights.so_what: Signals tightening basic-research funding in Europe, raising the bar and prestige for grants that are won.
insights.locator: The ERC Advanced Grant is extremely competitive

insights.claim: A fitness landscape maps every possible genetic variant, with genes one mutation apart as neighbors and elevation representing the fitness of organisms carrying each variant.
insights.speaker: Santa Fe Institute
insights.so_what: Core mental model for reasoning about how populations navigate genetic possibility space toward higher fitness.
insights.locator: Imagine every possible version of a gene laid out

insights.claim: Recent experiments show fitness landscapes are often rugged, with many peaks of varying height separated by valleys that natural selection cannot cross because selection can only move an organism uphill.
insights.speaker: Santa Fe Institute
insights.so_what: Implies populations should get trapped on mediocre local peaks — a finding that conflicts with observed high adaptation, framing the open research question SEASCAPES targets.
insights.quote: Natural selection is like a hiker who can only go uphill.
insights.locator: Recent experiments reveal that fitness landscapes are often rugged

insights.claim: There is an unresolved paradox in evolutionary theory: rugged fitness landscapes should trap organisms at local fitness maxima, yet Darwinian evolution nonetheless consistently produces highly adapted organisms.
insights.speaker: Santa Fe Institute
insights.so_what: This unresolved paradox is the scientific gap the ERC-funded research is designed to close, defining what a successful outcome would explain.
insights.locator: But Darwinian evolution nonetheless produces highly adapted organisms

insights.claim: Wagner hypothesizes that environmental change turns static fitness landscapes into dynamic 'seascapes,' where genetic variants gain and lose fitness as the environment shifts, potentially letting populations escape local peaks they would otherwise be stuck on.
insights.speaker: Santa Fe Institute
insights.so_what: If validated, reframes adaptation as environment-driven landscape reshaping rather than pure hill-climbing — relevant to any field modeling systems under shifting selective pressure, including antibiotic/drug resistance and protein engineering.
insights.locator: One possibility Wagner proposes is that environmental change creates dynamically changing landscapes

insights.claim: An alternative hypothesis Wagner is testing is that weakly selected or neutral mutations act as stepping stones, letting organisms cross otherwise-inaccessible valleys to reach higher nearby fitness peaks.
insights.speaker: Santa Fe Institute
insights.so_what: A competing explanatory mechanism the project will test against the seascape hypothesis; expect the eventual output to identify which (or what mix) actually drives adaptation.
insights.locator: Another possibility is that weakly selected or neutral mutations act like stepping stones

insights.claim: The SEASCAPES project will combine genome editing, E. coli growth-rate measurements, and AI to map up to 100 trillion genotypes across study systems including proteins and regulatory DNA.
insights.speaker: Santa Fe Institute
insights.so_what: Scale (100 trillion genotypes) signals a computationally intensive, AI-dependent empirical program — a template for how AI-driven high-throughput genotype mapping could be applied elsewhere in synthetic biology.
insights.locator: The researchers will use genome editing, growth rate measurements in E.coli

insights.claim: Wagner believes the SEASCAPES research could have significant downstream implications for medicine and protein engineering.
insights.speaker: Santa Fe Institute
insights.so_what: A basic-science bet with potential long-horizon translational payoff in drug design and engineered proteins, worth tracking as the five-year grant progresses.
insights.locator: Wagner believes this research could have strong implications
